skill.name: coordinating-projects
skill.description: Guides multi-project coordination, roadmap planning, dependency mapping, portfolio health reviews, and resource allocation across software teams.
skill.summary: Coordinate project portfolios, dependencies, roadmaps, and team capacity across multiple software initiatives.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and weak-cryptography hits. Review found the weak-cryptography hits were false positives in project-planning text, while Bash, GitHub CLI, local file reads, and file-copy examples are real but user-directed operational guidance. No prompt-injection text, credential theft, obfuscation, or malicious exfiltration pattern was found.

medium_findings.title: Broad External Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for external command usage, but not malicious. The skill instructs agents to run gh, find, cat, cp, jq, and python3 commands for portfolio discovery and document generation. These commands are legitimate for the skill purpose, but they can read local project files, write roadmap files, and run scripts if executed without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit in Bash code fences and align with the skill purpose. Confidence is high because the risk is real operational capability, but the reviewed text does not show command injection or malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: GitHub CLI Repository Enumeration
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE semantic network concern. The skill suggests gh commands that list organization repositories, issues, pull requests, and releases. This can expose private portfolio metadata through an authenticated GitHub session, although no unauthorized destination or credential exfiltration is present.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The GitHub CLI commands clearly perform network-backed repository discovery. The concern is moderate because the commands target GitHub APIs for a project-management workflow, not an unknown external endpoint.

medium_findings.title: Referenced Local Script Execution
medium_findings.description: NEEDS REVIEW for marketplace use. The skill describes running python3 scripts from a baseDir scripts folder, but the audited file structure contains only SKILL.md. If such scripts are supplied later, they need separate review before publication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script execution instructions are clear, but no script file exists in the reviewed package. The risk depends on future or external files, so confidence is medium-high rather than very high.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory Access Is Project Discovery
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious hidden-file access. The references to .git and .claude-project paths are used to discover repositories and store project-planning documents. This is expected for a portfolio coordination skill, but agents should still ask before scanning large workspaces or writing files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hidden paths are visible in planning and repository-discovery examples. The surrounding context confirms legitimate project-management use rather than stealthy collection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for weak cryptographic algorithms. The flagged lines contain metadata, planning guidance, roadmap examples, API dependency text, and risk template placeholders. No MD5, SHA1, encryption, hashing, or password-storage implementation was found in the reviewed SKILL.md content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review of the flagged contexts found only ordinary project-management language and Markdown examples. There is no crypto API, hash algorithm, or security-sensitive implementation in those locations.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Hits Are Planning Language
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for reconnaissance intent. The flagged lines describe a hybrid API strategy and avoiding resource over-allocation. They do not gather operating-system, network, host, or credential information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are plain strategic planning advice. They do not include reconnaissance commands or requests for sensitive system details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands in Skill Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes Bash examples for repository discovery, dependency inspection, copying templates, and running Python scripts. These should be treated as user-approved actions rather than automatic execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell snippets are explicit and repeat across the skill. They are operationally useful but require consent because they access repositories and write files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Networked GitHub Metadata Queries
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill suggests GitHub CLI commands that query repositories, issues, pull requests, and releases. This can disclose organization metadata to the active GitHub account context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The gh commands are plainly present and network-backed. The destination is GitHub, so this is a controlled tool risk rather than evidence of malicious exfiltration.

content.value_statement: Teams often lose visibility when many projects share people, dates, and dependencies. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code organize roadmaps, risks, releases, and capacity decisions across a portfolio.
actual_capabilities: Maps dependencies between projects, teams, releases, and shared infrastructure.
Builds quarterly, annual, now-next-later, theme-based, and initiative-based roadmap structures.
Compares resource allocation options for dedicated teams, pooled teams, and matrix assignments.
Creates portfolio health summaries with status, progress, blockers, budget, and utilization fields.
Plans coordinated releases with sequencing, checkpoints, monitoring, rollback, and communication steps.
Delegates tactical execution, research, and quality review to other named skills when available.
limitations: The package only includes SKILL.md; referenced templates and scripts are not present in the audited files.
GitHub CLI examples require an authenticated GitHub account and user approval before execution.
Recommendations depend on accurate project data, team capacity, priorities, and stakeholder constraints.
It provides planning guidance, not a replacement for project management systems or governance decisions.

use_cases.title: Plan a quarterly portfolio roadmap
use_cases.description: Turn company goals, active initiatives, and team capacity into a clear quarterly roadmap with dependencies and milestones.

use_cases.title: Coordinate a multi-service release
use_cases.description: Sequence API, web, mobile, and infrastructure releases with integration checkpoints, communication plans, and rollback paths.

use_cases.title: Resolve resource conflicts across teams
use_cases.description: Compare allocation options when teams are over capacity, projects are blocked, or new hires need onboarding plans.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize portfolio status
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to summarize our active projects. Identify status, owners, key dependencies, blockers, and next decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Create a quarterly roadmap
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create a quarterly roadmap from these goals, projects, team capacities, and deadlines. Include themes, milestones, dependencies, and risks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a coordinated release
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to coordinate a release across these projects. Define sequence, checkpoints, integration testing, monitoring, rollback, and stakeholder communication.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize resource allocation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to evaluate resource allocation options across this portfolio. Compare tradeoffs, critical path impact, team load, onboarding cost, and recommended sequencing.

output_examples.input: Create a Q2 roadmap for seven projects across three teams.
output_examples.output: A roadmap grouped by strategic themes, with project milestones, capacity allocation, dependency notes, risks, and executive summary items.

output_examples.input: Coordinate the API, web, and mobile release next month.
output_examples.output: A release plan with deployment order, integration checkpoints, monitoring responsibilities, customer communication, and rollback guidance.

output_examples.input: Two engineers are joining while one critical project is behind.
output_examples.output: A resource allocation recommendation comparing options, onboarding constraints, schedule impact, and follow-up review points.

best_practices: Confirm scope, repositories, and allowed commands before gathering project data.
Keep dependency maps and roadmaps current as priorities, staffing, and timelines change.
Separate strategic coordination from tactical execution, and assign clear owners for each dependency.
anti_patterns: Running GitHub or filesystem discovery commands without user approval.
Treating example templates or scripts as available when the package does not include them.
Allocating people to many simultaneous projects without accounting for context switching.

faq.question: What does this skill help coordinate?
faq.answer: It helps coordinate roadmaps, dependencies, releases, resources, portfolio health, and strategic priorities across multiple projects.

faq.question: Does this skill include working scripts or templates?
faq.answer: No. The audited package contains SKILL.md only, although the text references templates and scripts.

faq.question: Can it access private GitHub repositories?
faq.answer: Only if the user approves GitHub CLI commands and the active environment has authenticated access.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive project portfolios?
faq.answer: Use it with care. Review commands before execution and avoid sharing confidential roadmap details outside approved systems.

faq.question: Which AI tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: It is best for engineering leaders, product managers, program managers, and release managers coordinating several projects.
